{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Projected Mortality Impacts","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Purpose:
Estimate the potential impact of service coverage reductions on maternal and child mortality under different funding and disruption scenarios.

# How it works:
- Uses the Lives Saved Tool (LiST), which links intervention coverage to maternal and child mortality outcomes.
- Applies funding scenarios (e.g., loss of USAID, loss of USAID + WB, or 20–50% coverage reductions) to project changes in intervention coverage.
- Incorporates the distribution of investments across government, donors, and other partners into the secnario development.

# Measuring impact:
- LiST calculates the additional maternal and child deaths expected under each scenario compared to the baseline.
- Results are shown for different time horizons (e.g., immediate losses in 2025 vs. compounding effects in 2026 and beyond).

# How to interpret figures:
- Lines or bars show projected deaths under different disruption scenarios.
- Scenarios are illustrative, not predictive — they highlight the scale of potential risk under different funding assumptions."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Analyze historical health service utilization trends and project future scenarios based on different growth patterns.

# How it works:
Calculates Compound Annual Growth Rates (CAGR) for key health indicators across three distinct periods: Post-Ebola recovery (2017-2019), COVID-19 disruption (2020-2022), and IFISH accelerated implementation (2023-2025).
Projects five-year scenarios (2026-2030) using multiple approaches:
- Apply observed growth rates from each period
- Model vaccine supply chain disruption (-19.4% annually, based on 2021-2022 BCG stockout)
- Model workforce attrition impact on maternal health services (-15% annually)

# Measuring impact:
- CAGR values quantify annual growth or decline rates for each health service during different periods.
- Projection scenarios show potential service volumes under continued implementation of different policy approaches.

# How to interpret figures
- Solid lines show actual historical service volumes; dashed and dotted lines show potential future scenarios based on different growth assumptions.
- Scenarios model potential futures under different assumptions. They inform planning and policy discussions rather than predict definitive outcomes."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":3,"markdown":"Both malaria indicators show frequent >10% quarter-to-quarter swings. RDT+ is highly variable, and treatment in less than 24 hrs follows a similar pattern at a lower level. 

The largest percent increase was in Q2 2025 (RDT+ +55%, treatment +49%). Patterns are seasonal; cases typically peak in Q2.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Track changes in health service use over time, identifying where services fall below or rise above expected patterns.

# How it works:
- Uses routine HMIS data, cleaned for outliers and missing values.
- Builds an “expected” trend line for each service, adjusting for seasonality and historical trends in service utilization.
- Compares actual service volumes to expected levels.


# Measuring impact:
- Flagged disruption periods are analyzed to estimate how much service volumes changed compared to what was expected.
- Results are shown at nationaland sub-national levels highlighting both system-wide and localized effects.


# How to interpret figures
- Red shaded areas = potential disruptions (service volumes lower than expected).
- Green shaded areas = potential surpluses (service volumes higher than expected).
- These are signals, not conclusions — they highlight when and where volumes deviate, but require further investigation into the underlying reasons (“why”)."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":4,"markdown":"Antenatal care first visit: In early 2025, volumes are declining compared to late 2024 peaks, though no major disruptions are flagged yet.

Antenatal care fourth visit: In early 2025, visits are above expected levels, with a surplus flagged.

ANC4 showed notable surpluses in Q2 2025, peaking at +12.7% in April (≈1,300 additional visits nationally) and remaining 6–7% above expected in May–June.


As 2025 begins, ANC1 shows a downward shift while ANC4 exceeds expectations, underscoring the need to closely monitor whether changes in the health sector affect early contact and continuity of care differently."},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"40deda13-d17b-458c-9e66-60325cb6e546","moduleId":"m003","selectedReplicantValue":"","isDefault":false}}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"2025 shows declining ANC1 but rising ANC4, highlighting differences in initial contact vs. continued care"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – Sept 2025)

Overall completeness is high: Most indicators remain above 90% throughout the period, showing reliable reporting across the HMIS.

Areas of weaker completeness: Family planning and PNC indicators show dips into the 80–89% range at times, particularly mid-2023 and mid-2024.

Improvement in 2025: Completeness is consistently strong across nearly all indicators (>95%), reducing concern about missing reports.

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis

Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low, expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.9,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"dea512fb-3370-4c50-aff1-7923ed1107e3","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":3,"markdown":"# Bong County","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"f5f71762-0536-4d1d-9553-ea5930327b3a","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"replicateBy":"admin_area_2","selectedReplicantValue":"Bong"}}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":""}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"image","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgFile":"20033631845_83350bddf9_o.jpg","imgFit":"cover","markdown":""},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Sustaining essential health services during periods of funding uncertainty and program change requires timely evidence.

The FASTR approach uses routine HMIS data to track service delivery trends and project how utilization may evolve under different implementation and funding scenarios. This forward-looking perspective helps anticipate where service delivery could accelerate, stall, or decline.

In addition, we use the Lives Saved Tool (LiST) to project the potential mortality impacts of funding losses and coverage reductions. This modelling complements the utilization analysis by showing the consequences for maternal and child survival if disruptions are not addressed.

Together, these analyses provide a timely, system-wide perspective that highlights shifts in service use and translates them into actionable insights on what those changes could mean for health outcomes."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Tracking Disruptions in Essential Services Using HMIS Data","useFooter":false,"footerLogos":[]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"image","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgFile":"report_image1 (2).png","imgFit":"cover"},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"The objective of this report is to provide a timely snapshot of health sector performance by assessing key RMNCAH+N service utilization metrics and HMIS data quality.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness.

# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":4,"markdown":"BCG: Sustained disruptions through 2024, with volumes dipping below expectations, but steady recovery into mid-2025 (overall –0.7% Jan–Jun).

Penta1 & Penta3: Also showed shortfalls in 2024, followed by a clear rebound in 2025, with large June surpluses (+20% and +16%).

Despite early 2025 shortfalls, immunization indicators are near expected levels by mid-2025, with signs of strengthening service continuity.

The parallel recovery across BCG, Penta1, and Penta3 suggests a real system-wide rebound in immunization, not just random fluctuation."},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.8,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"2ac52493-e791-4f7f-9c28-fe5a3520d216","moduleId":"m003","selectedReplicantValue":"","isDefault":false}}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Despite widespread shortfalls in 2024, immunization services show signs of recovery by mid-2025"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness. Mortality indicators are excluded from adjustment.

# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"section","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"Annex 2: Methodology","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"placeholder","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1}]],"useHeader":true}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}
